comprehensive_summary:
Guy de Maupassant was a French short story writer and novelist, a protege of Gustave Flaubert and a leading figure of French Naturalism. The Goncourt brothers' journal started a rumor that he was Flaubert's illegitimate son. He went mad from syphilis late in life, a fact reflected in his horror story 'The Horla.' Isaac Babel wrote a story titled 'Guy de Maupassant' in which a translator recalls how he went mad and began eating his own feces.

His most famous story is 'The Necklace,' about Mathilde Loisel, the wife of a clerk, who borrows a piece of jewelry from Madame Forestier for a ball. She loses it and spends ten years working to pay off the debt for a replacement, only to discover in a twist ending that the original was made of paste and worth only 500 francs. Many stories were printed in Gil Blas. Henry James wrote 'Paste' as a reversal of 'The Necklace.'

'Boule de Suif' ('Ball of Fat') is about the prostitute Elisabeth Rousset, who shares her chickens (jellied, 'cut into joints') with fellow passengers on a stagecoach from Rouen to Le Havre during the Franco-Prussian War. The other passengers, including the Loiseaus, Count de Breville, and Cornudet, convince her to sleep with a Prussian officer so they can continue their journey. At the end, the red-bearded Cornudet whistles the Marseillaise while Boule de Suif weeps. It was among six stories collected in Evenings at Medan, alongside works by Huysmans and others.

'Mademoiselle Fifi' features a Prussian officer given a feminine nickname due to his use of the phrase 'fi donc.' He blows up a statue of Venus with a teapot filled with gunpowder, shoots out the eyes of a portrait on which he has drawn a mustache, and makes a speech mocking France. The Jewish prostitute Rachel stabs him through the neck with a dessert (cheese) knife and hides in a church bell tower, ringing the bells until German troops depart.

Bel-Ami is Maupassant's most famous novel, about the handsome ex-soldier Georges Duroy, a journalist at La Vie Francaise who climbs the social ranks through affairs with wealthy women. Madeleine ghostwrites journal articles for him. He fights an incompetent duel with a gossip columnist from La Plume. He makes a fortune from insider trading in Morocco. Tolstoy claimed to find 'no moral relation' in it.

'The Horla' is written as diary entries beginning 'May 8th.' The narrator waves to a Brazilian ship and begins experiencing a vampire spirit's possession. He burns down his house with servants inside to kill the creature, concluding 'He is not dead... I suppose I must kill myself.' A monk compares the supernatural to unseen wind. Maupassant's syphilitic madness may be reflected in the story.

'Two Friends' features an officer who emotionlessly cooks the fish caught by two executed fishermen (a watchmaker and draper) who refused to give up a password; they are shot and left to float downstream. A.J. Greimas wrote a 300-page semiotic analysis of this six-page story.

Pierre et Jean is about two brothers; an inheritance from Marechal leads a doctor to suspect his brother's illegitimacy. It opens on a retired jeweler's boat. Maupassant considered it his best novel and wrote the preface 'The Novel,' listing 25 varied novels to refute critics.

Une Vie follows a baron's daughter from her teenage exit from a convent to her death; a jealous husband pushes a moveable hut containing his wife and her lover off a cliff.

'Mother Savage' tells of a woman who records Prussian soldiers' addresses, traps them in a loft, and sets it on fire after learning her son died in battle. 'The Piece of String' features Maitre Hauchecorne, who picks up the title object and is accused by a harness-maker of stealing a pocketbook. 'My Uncle Jules' involves discovering on a steamboat that a poor man selling oysters is the narrator's brother. 'Miss Harriet' features an English woman who drowns in a well after the narrator, a painter, breaks her heart. 'Madame Tellier's Establishment' has a group of prostitutes making an entire church congregation cry at a First Communion ceremony. 'Useless Beauty' has a countess claiming her husband did not father one of her seven children. 'The Hand' features a judge visiting the reclusive Englishman John Howell, who has a severed hand chained in his gun room. 'Dr. Heraclius Gloss' features a man going insane from reading about metempsychosis. 'The Terror' has a narrator hallucinating a man in his armchair.
discarded_note: Discarded T1 about Isaac Babel/Singer (Maupassant is only a passing reference).

works.name: "The Necklace"
works.description: Maupassant's most famous story. Mathilde Loisel borrows jewelry from Madame Forestier, loses it, works ten years to replace it, only to learn the original was paste. The primary giveaway for Maupassant.

clues.clue: Mathilde Loisel borrows jewelry from Madame Forestier, loses it at a ball, works ten years to pay it off.
quotes: Mathilde Loisel and her husband work to pay off their debt after buying a replacement piece of jewelry
a husband and wife work ten years to recover the funds to pay for the lost title object

clues.clue: Twist ending: the original was worth only 500 francs / made of paste.
quotes: Madame Forestier reveals that it was made of paste
the original necklace that Mathilde borrowed was actually an imitation made of this material

clues.clue: Henry James wrote 'Paste' as a reversal.
quotes: had his most famous story inverted in Henry James's 'Paste'

clues.clue: Many of his stories were printed in Gil Blas.
quotes: one of this author's many stories printed in Gil Blas

works.name: "Boule de Suif"
works.description: About the prostitute Elisabeth Rousset on a stagecoach during the Franco-Prussian War. Fellow passengers convince her to sleep with a Prussian officer. Published in Evenings at Medan.

clues.clue: Elisabeth Rousset shares chickens with passengers on a stagecoach from Rouen to Le Havre; the Loiseaus and Count de Breville convince her to sleep with a Prussian officer.
quotes: a group of people in a stagecoach who try to get the title rotund prostitute to sleep with a Prussian officer
Count de Breville persuades the title prostitute to sleep with a Prussian officer

clues.clue: Cornudet whistles the Marseillaise at the end while Boule de Suif weeps.
quotes: Cornudet whistles the Marseillaise after Elizabeth Rousset sleeps with a Prussian officer
a red-bearded man...whistling a national anthem while the title character weeps

clues.clue: The chickens are 'cut into joints' and 'imbedded in jelly'; Loiseau wants to partake.
quotes: These objects are 'cut into joints' and 'imbedded in jelly'

clues.clue: A nun proclaims 'the end justifies the means'; nuns arrange a 'plan of campaign.'
quotes: a nun proclaiming 'the end justifies the means'

clues.clue: Published in Evenings at Medan alongside stories by Huysmans and others.
quotes: among the six stories by different Naturalists collected in Evenings at Medan

works.name: "Mademoiselle Fifi"
works.description: A Prussian officer with an effeminate nickname blows up a Venus statue and is stabbed by the prostitute Rachel during the Franco-Prussian War.

clues.clue: The Jewish prostitute Rachel stabs the title officer through the neck with a dessert/cheese knife and hides in a bell tower, ringing bells.
quotes: Rachel grabs a dessert knife and sticks it in the throat of an officer with an effeminate nickname
Rachel rings a church bell after stabbing an arrogant German captain with a cheese knife

clues.clue: The officer blows up a statue of Venus with a teapot filled with gunpowder; shoots out the eyes of a portrait with a mustache drawn on it.
quotes: a character who blows up a statue of Venus using a teapot filled with gunpowder
this author created a man who...blows tobacco smoke down her throat, as well as a man who shoots out the eyes of a portrait

clues.clue: Nicknamed 'Mademoiselle Fifi' due to his use of the phrase 'fi donc.'
quotes: This man wrote a story in which the protagonist tells the title character 'I am not a woman, I am only a strumpet!'
A character created by this author whose use of the phrase 'fi donc' has earned him a feminine nickname

works.name: Bel-Ami
works.description: Maupassant's most famous novel about the journalist Georges Duroy climbing society through affairs. Madeleine ghostwrites for him.

clues.clue: The handsome ex-soldier Georges Duroy rises through society using affairs with wealthy women at the newspaper La Vie Francaise.
quotes: A series of affairs helps a journalist climb the ranks of the newspaper La Vie Francaise
a handsome ex-soldier named Georges Duroy

clues.clue: Madeleine ghostwrites journal articles for Duroy.
quotes: Madeleine ghostwrites journal articles for a handsome ex-soldier named Georges Duroy

clues.clue: Duroy fights an incompetent duel with a gossip columnist from La Plume; makes a fortune from insider trading in Morocco.
quotes: a character fights an incompetent duel with a gossip columnist from La Plume
a character makes a fortune off insider trading in Morocco

clues.clue: Tolstoy claimed to find 'no moral relation' in it.
quotes: Tolstoy claimed to find 'no moral relation' in Maupassant's most famous novel

works.name: "The Horla"
works.description: A horror story in diary form about a narrator possessed by a vampire spirit from a Brazilian ship. Reflects Maupassant's syphilitic madness.

clues.clue: Written as diary entries beginning 'May 8th'; narrator waves to a Brazilian ship and is possessed by a vampire spirit.
quotes: In that story by this author, which is framed as diary entries beginning 'May 8th'
the protagonist first meets the title thing after waving to a Brazilian ship

clues.clue: Narrator burns down his house with servants inside; concludes 'He is not dead... I suppose I must kill myself.'
quotes: the protagonist burns down his house with his servants still inside while under the sway of a vampire spirit
concludes 'He is not dead... I suppose I must kill myself'

clues.clue: Narrator covers his mouth with black lead, seals water and milk before bed; both are gone in the morning.
quotes: The narrator of this story covers his mouth with black lead and seals a carafe of water and a glass of milk

clues.clue: Reflects Maupassant's own syphilitic madness.
quotes: This author's struggle with syphilitic madness may be reflected

works.name: Other Stories and Novels
works.description: Various other works including 'Two Friends,' Pierre et Jean, Une Vie, 'Mother Savage,' 'The Piece of String,' 'My Uncle Jules,' 'Miss Harriet,' 'Madame Tellier's Establishment,' and biographical details.

clues.clue: 'Two Friends': an officer cooks fish caught by two executed fishermen who refused to give up a password. A.J. Greimas wrote a 300-page analysis.
quotes: an officer emotionlessly cooks the fish caught by an executed watchmaker and a draper who are shot and left to float downstream
A six-page story by this author is exhaustively analyzed in a 300-page book by A.J. Greimas

clues.clue: Pierre et Jean: an inheritance from Marechal leads a doctor to suspect his brother's illegitimacy; considered his best novel. Preface 'The Novel' lists 25 novels to refute critics.
quotes: an inheritance from Marechal leads a doctor to suspect his brother's illegitimacy
This protege of Gustave Flaubert considered Pierre et Jean his best novel.

clues.clue: Une Vie: a baron's daughter from convent exit to death; a jealous husband pushes a hut off a cliff.
quotes: follows the disappointing life of a baron's daughter from her teenage exit from a convent to her death
a jealous husband pushes a moveable hut containing his wife and her lover off a cliff

clues.clue: 'Mother Savage': records Prussian soldiers' addresses; traps them in a loft and sets fire after her son's death.
quotes: the title woman setting fire to her own home in order to kill young men
the title character responds to the news that her son has died in battle by trapping Prussian soldiers in a loft

clues.clue: 'The Piece of String': Maitre Hauchecorne picks up string and is accused of stealing a pocketbook.
quotes: Maitre Hauchecorne is ostracized for stealing money thanks to a harness-maker's lie that the title object was a pocketbook

clues.clue: 'Madame Tellier's Establishment': prostitutes make a church congregation cry at a First Communion.
quotes: a group of prostitutes makes an entire church congregation cry at a confirmation ceremony

clues.clue: 'My Uncle Jules': a man on a steamboat discovers a poor man selling oysters is his brother.
quotes: on a steamboat, discovers that a poor man selling oysters is his brother

clues.clue: 'Miss Harriet': an English admirer drowns in a well after the narrator breaks her heart.
quotes: Miss Harriet drowns in a well after the narrator, a painter, breaks her heart

clues.clue: Protege of Gustave Flaubert; Goncourt brothers spread rumor he was Flaubert's son.
quotes: This protege of Gustave Flaubert
The Goncourts initiated a story that this author was actually Flaubert's son

clues.clue: Went mad from syphilis; the Goncourt journal notes he was 'proud (at long last!)' to have caught 'the magnificent pox.'
quotes: this author was 'proud (at long last!)' to have caught the 'magnificent pox' that is 'syphilis!'

cards.front: Short Story: Henry James wrote 'Paste' as a reversal of this story about Mathilde Loisel working ten years to replace lost borrowed jewelry
Short Story: a nun proclaims 'the end justifies the means' while passengers convince the title prostitute Elisabeth Rousset to sleep with a Prussian officer
Short Story: Cornudet whistles the Marseillaise at the end while the title character weeps; published in Evenings at Medan
Short Story: the title officer blows up a statue of Venus with a gunpowder-filled teapot; shoots out the eyes of a portrait he drew a mustache on
Short Story: Rachel stabs the title officer through the neck with a dessert knife and hides in a bell tower, ringing the bells
Novel: Madeleine ghostwrites for the journalist Georges Duroy at La Vie Francaise; Duroy makes a fortune from insider trading in Morocco
Novel: Duroy fights an incompetent duel with a gossip columnist from La Plume; Tolstoy found 'no moral relation' in it
Short Story: diary entries beginning 'May 8th'; narrator waves to a Brazilian ship and is possessed by a vampire spirit; burns his house with servants inside
Short Story: narrator seals water and milk before bed; both are gone in the morning; concludes 'He is not dead... I suppose I must kill myself'
Short Story: an officer emotionlessly cooks fish caught by two executed fishermen who refused to give up a password; A.J. Greimas wrote a 300-page semiotic analysis
Novel: an inheritance from Marechal leads a doctor to suspect his brother's illegitimacy; the author considered it his best novel and wrote the preface 'The Novel'
Novel: a jealous husband pushes a moveable hut off a cliff; follows a baron's daughter from convent exit to death
Short Story: the title character records Prussian soldiers' addresses, traps them in a loft, and sets fire after learning her son died in battle
Short Story: Maitre Hauchecorne picks up the title object and is falsely accused of stealing a pocketbook by a harness-maker
Short Story: a group of prostitutes makes an entire church congregation cry at a First Communion ceremony
Author: the Goncourt brothers' journal noted this author was 'proud (at long last!)' to have caught 'the magnificent pox' of syphilis

recursive_suggestions: "Boule de Suif" (as standalone answerline, Literature filter)
summary: Guy de Maupassant was a French short story writer and novelist, a protege of Gustave Flaubert and a leading figure of French Naturalism.
